words.definition: the title at the top of a newspaper article
example_sentences: The story made the front page headlines.
She always reads the headlines over breakfast.
The headline was misleading — the article told a different story.
words.pronunciation_tip: HED-lyn — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the title of the news' — say 'the headline'.

words.definition: to transmit a programme on radio or television
example_sentences: The speech was broadcast live on national TV.
The documentary was first broadcast in 2022.
A live broadcast reaches millions simultaneously.
words.pronunciation_tip: BRAWD-kast — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'send on TV' — say 'broadcast'.

words.definition: the suppression of parts of communication deemed unacceptable
example_sentences: Press censorship is a sign of an authoritarian government.
Many journalists have been imprisoned for avoiding censorship.
Internet censorship is practised in several countries.
words.pronunciation_tip: SEN-ser-ship — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the government controls the news' as a noun — say 'censorship'.

words.definition: the number of copies of a newspaper sold
example_sentences: The newspaper has a daily circulation of 200,000.
Print circulation has fallen dramatically in the digital age.
High circulation means more advertising revenue.
words.pronunciation_tip: sur-kyoo-LAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'number of newspapers sold' every time — say 'circulation'.

words.definition: an article expressing the opinion of the newspaper's editor
example_sentences: The editorial criticised the government's decision.
She wrote an editorial demanding action on climate.
The editorial board decides the newspaper's position.
words.pronunciation_tip: ed-ih-TAW-ree-ul — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the opinion article from the editor' — say 'the editorial'.

words.definition: treating all sides fairly without favouritism
example_sentences: A journalist must be impartial and report all sides.
The BBC aims to be impartial in its reporting.
An impartial investigation is essential for trust.
words.pronunciation_tip: im-PAR-shul — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'fair and balanced' always — say 'impartial'.

words.definition: spreading very quickly to many people online
example_sentences: The video went viral and got 10 million views overnight.
A viral post can reach more people than a TV ad.
Her accidental viral moment changed her life completely.
words.pronunciation_tip: VY-rul — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the video became popular everywhere quickly' — say 'it went viral'.

words.definition: deliberately false information spread to deceive people
example_sentences: Disinformation about vaccines spread rapidly online.
Governments have struggled to combat disinformation.
Disinformation campaigns can undermine democracy.
words.pronunciation_tip: dis-in-fawr-MAY-shun — six syllables, stress on fourth.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse disinformation (deliberate) with misinformation (accidental). Both are wrong info.

words.definition: the right of journalists to report without government control
example_sentences: Press freedom is under threat in many countries.
Reporters Without Borders ranks countries by press freedom.
Without press freedom, corruption goes unchallenged.
words.pronunciation_tip: PRESS FREE-dum.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'freedom to write in newspapers' — say 'press freedom'.
words.collocations: ['protect press freedom', 'threats to press freedom', 'press freedom index']

words.definition: an exclusive news story obtained before other media
example_sentences: The journalist got the scoop on the resignation.
It was the biggest scoop of her career.
Every reporter wants to get the scoop.
words.pronunciation_tip: SKOOP — one syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'an exclusive news story' every time — say 'a scoop'.

words.definition: a person who gives a journalist information
example_sentences: The journalist protected her source even when pressured.
Always verify information from multiple sources.
The source requested anonymity.
words.pronunciation_tip: SAWSS — one syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the person who told the journalist' — say 'the source'.

words.definition: a payment for regular access to a publication or service
example_sentences: She has a digital subscription to two newspapers.
The subscription model has replaced single-copy sales.
Netflix is a subscription streaming service.
words.pronunciation_tip: sub-SKRIP-shun — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I pay monthly for the newspaper' — say 'I have a subscription'.

words.definition: online content designed to attract clicks using sensational titles
example_sentences: The article was pure clickbait — the headline was misleading.
Clickbait undermines trust in online journalism.
Don't be fooled by clickbait headlines.
words.pronunciation_tip: KLIK-bayt — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a fake exciting title' — say 'clickbait'.

words.definition: a set of rules that determines what content you see online
example_sentences: Social media algorithms favour emotionally charged content.
The algorithm changes mean fewer people see organic posts.
Understanding the algorithm is key to social media success.
words.pronunciation_tip: AL-guh-rith-um — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the computer decides what I see' — say 'the algorithm'.

words.definition: photographers who follow celebrities to take pictures for sale
example_sentences: The paparazzi were waiting outside the hotel.
She was followed by paparazzi everywhere she went.
Being constantly followed by paparazzi is exhausting.
words.pronunciation_tip: pap-uh-RAT-see — four syllables, stress on third. Plural form even when one.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'celebrity photographers' in casual speech — say 'paparazzi'.

words.definition: a person with a large social media following who promotes products
example_sentences: She became a major influencer after her cooking videos went viral.
Brands pay influencers millions to promote their products.
The rise of the influencer has changed marketing.
words.pronunciation_tip: IN-floo-un-ser — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'famous person on social media' — say 'influencer'.

words.definition: a journalist who reports from a specific location or on a topic
example_sentences: The foreign correspondent reported from Kyiv.
She is the BBC's Middle East correspondent.
He worked as a war correspondent for twenty years.
words.pronunciation_tip: kor-ih-SPON-dunt — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the journalist who is in another country' — say 'the correspondent'.

words.definition: the use of humour or irony to criticise people or events
example_sentences: The show uses satire to mock political figures.
Political satire has a long history in journalism.
The article was meant as satire but many readers missed the irony.
words.pronunciation_tip: SAT-yr — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'funny criticism of important people' every time — say 'satire'.

words.definition: the name of the journalist who wrote the article
example_sentences: Check the byline to see who wrote the story.
She got her first byline in a national newspaper at 24.
The byline was missing — it was an anonymous piece.
words.pronunciation_tip: BY-lyn — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the name of the journalist' every time — say 'the byline'.

words.definition: the story or explanation given about events
example_sentences: The government tried to control the narrative.
Different media outlets presented very different narratives.
The documentary challenges the official narrative.
words.pronunciation_tip: NAIR-uh-tiv — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the story people tell about something' every time — say 'the narrative'.
